The meeting, or rather meectings, in Dublin on Mon-
day night, in connection with the Dirty Papers’ Cru-
sade, were very hearty and encouraging demonstra-
tions, though we cannot quite agree with the Lord
Mayor when he said that the city of Dublin had made
up its mind on the question once and for all. Dirty
“papers are still sold openly in Dublin, and on the streets
on Sundays. Lord Aberdeen spoke strongly at the
meeting, and we do not think that anyone will question
his zeal and sincerity against dirty publications, how-
ever they may agree or disagree with his political and
other views, In the course of his remarks he said:

You show me two men: you point to one and

say that is the man who stole your purse, and then

you point to the other and say that is the man
who gas circulated a pernicious picture or book.

Then say I—'‘ Let me shake hands with the pick-
E %;e%.

ocket as a sort of gentleman compared with the

”

That was direct and to the point, and it shows that Lord
Aberdeen would prefer the hand-shake of a pickpocket
rather than that of very many newsagents, big and
small, throughout this country. = As one working defini-
tion we would define a dirty paper as a paper that con-
tained dirty advertisements. There are, no doubt,
many papers and publications that are dirty, though
they may not contain any advertisements or any dirty
advertisements; but the presence of dirty advertise-
ments, indicating where dirty pamphlets. etc., may
be had, constitute a publication a dirty one, however
otherwise reputable it may be. Some months ago we saw
a newsagent’s shop showing numbers of a publication
that is, we believe, fairly widely sold in Dublin.  That
newsagent, we understand, had fallen in with the sug-
gestions of the Vigilance Committee. ~We never saw
a copy of the paper beyond seeing it in newsagents’
shops, but recently a clergyman told us that it contained
several most objectionable advertisements, and was,
therefore, a dirty paper. Every paper that contained
these advertisements should be marked down as a dirty
paper. is a serious matter for the Vigilance Com-
mittees throughout- Ireland, for ignorant newsagents
may be inclined to regard everything as clean that is
not specifically pointed out to them as dirty, and so
the sale of a dirty paper that is not brought to the
attention of newsagents may be stimulated by the very
fact that it is not specifically banned by a Vigilance
Committee.

We feel sure that the meeting and the overflow meet-
ing on Monday will give a great fillip to the fight against
divty papers, but the work must go plodding along every
week in the year all over the country if the evil is per-
manently to be kept in check as much as possible.

A KETTLE IN HOT WATER.
Scene—A part of Stephen’s Green Park.
(Enter Professor Kettle).

Kettle—Ts this great Skeffy whom I see before me,
With whiskers waving in the morning breeze
Like banner of defiance. My eyes deceive me,
And did mistake a man of gentle peace
For that grim parlour Anarchist whose deeds
Make tea-cups tremble in the vengeful hands
Of maid’s emancipated. How is it that of late
The sudden sight of knickerbockers doth
Make Kettle start and tremble. It cannot be
That I do fear grim Skeffy with his beard
And other dark appendages of war,

Although in dread antagonism now
He takes the field against me. Perish the thought.
Let him approach like rugged Russian bear,
And Kettle with a proud and lofty calm,
Like ice-cold water from Olympic heights,
Shall quench his torrid rage.
Though I throw down no battle gage.
I am a man of mettle,
And Skeffy’s high tea-fighting rage,
Will never frighten Kettle.

(Enter Skeffy from behind a tree.)

Skeffy—Thou man of metal base—

Kettle—Help, help, oh, angels v
And ministers of gracé.

Skeffy—Ha | dost thou tremble,
Thou hollow-sounding instrument of tin.
Now, now thou feelest what terrific shockers
Are Skeffy’s whiskers and his knickerbockers.

Kettle—Proud ‘boastful'man,
Thy sudden’ apparition made me starb
And through my nerves sent thrilling waves of fear,
But now is Kettle’s mettle back again
To bravelthy visage with a front of brass.
I tell thee, Skefty, ere this thing we settle,
_Thow’rt bub  tinker to an M. T. Kettle.
Skeffy—Unchivalrous economist,
Vile sophister and calculator base,
I here do tell thee to thy brazen face,
Thou art a recreant knight, a traitor false
Unto the sacred cause of women's rights,
Which charge, I here declare myself prepared
Upon thy craven body to maintain
If thou wilt meet me in the Pheenix Park
In shock of clashing battle.
Kettle—Thy foul aspersions in a double lump
T hurl back into thy dastard beard,
And in the Phenix Park in iron cased
Will Kettle pouring with full steam ahead
Meet Skeffy with the dread colliding crash of
boiling rage.
Skeffy—I’ll knock smoke out of thee.
Kettle—Base' caitiff, I defy thee.
(Enter Cruso).
Cruso—Here stand I too,
A valiant champion of the Suffragettes,
Who'll dare in combat to unsheath a sword
Against the bosom of ' the dastard false
To window-breakers’ rights. - Show me the knave,
Who's guilty of that base and craven charge,
And he shall answer to the smiting sword
Of Trogan Cruso.
«Kettle—Go challenge Padraig Colum.
Cruso—T will.

(Enter a policeman).

Pol.—What’s all this row about?
You chaw the rag as if you meant to fight
‘Like Cuffe Sthreet warriors brave.
Kettle—Oh, this doth knock the Kettle bft the fire.
We are but human after all, and so
“We will disperse in peace. :

Exeunt peaceably).

* We see that Mr. P. J. Brady, M.P., has been made a
divector of the Midland Great Western Railway.
Strangely enough a few days before the appointment was
announced we had a complaint of bigotry against the
Company. We know that -it is hard to prove
individual cases of bigotry; bigotry in the lump,
as in the case of the Great Sourface Rail-
way, when we tcok. up the matter years ago,
where out of 49 or 47 of the higher officials, only two
were Catholics, needs no proof beyond the statement of P
the facts; but individual cases are hard and sometimes K \
impossible to prove. ~We trust that there will be no N
instances of anti-Catholic bigotry on the Mid-
land Great Western Railway, and the election of Mr. P.
J. Brady, M.P.—though as a fighter against bigotry
we could wish he were more robust—is a move in the
Trish and popular direction. We have also heard of
a complaint against the City of Dublin” Steampacket
Company.  Some time ago a vacancy occurred for an
assistant foreman in this Company’s store at the North
Wall.  Applications were not invited from the store
checkers, one of whom is invariably promoted to the
position. However, a checker who happened. to be of
the right colour, was appointed. This man had
only four years’ continuous service—he had been pre-
viously in the service for some four years, but we
believe resigned to take up another job. No less than
sixteen Catholics, of services varying from six to thirty
years, were passed over; and we understand there is
no question of superior ability in the matter, as all
the Catholics are quite equal to the promotion. The
Catholics, we understand, memoralised the managing
director and placed the facts before him, but no notice
was taken of the memorial. It must have a very para-
lysing ‘effect on young Catholics who get in, or force
their way in by competitive examination, to the ser-
vice of big public companies if they have reason to
assume, that whatever their character and merits may
be, that promotion is not for them:

Under the heading ** Civil War in Ulster.—Great
Orange Victory at Lisburn,”” ““ Orange LLamb ** writes
to us:—** Some days ago the brave ‘ Protestant Boys’
of the loyal town of Lisburn gained a great victory over
some of the Popish forces there. ~ The nuns had the
impertinence to get up an excursion for the children
they had been teaching all the year. Such an outrage
committed in open daylight in free Protestant Ulster
could not be allowed to pass unnoticed, so the stalwarts
of law and order gathered in force, and the cars as they
were passing out of the town were assailed with a well-
directed fusillade of stones and mud. The nuns and b}
little girls made no attempt to reply, but that is not to
be wondered at, for Papists are cowards all the world
over.  This victory over the forces of disloyalty and
disunion encouraged the Orangemen so much that they
prepared. for another battle in the evening.  There is
nothing like grim determination in the face of danger, |
as we say to one another in Ulster. So when the ex-
cursion returned to town it was met with a truly mag-
nificent fusillade of stones and mud.  The dresses of

Mr. M. Flanagan writes with reference to Mr. R. J.
Kelly’s recent article on ‘‘ Pipers "’ :—'* Mr. Kelly is
apparently an earnest advocate of the new Irish war
pipe. I say ‘new,’ because the instrument as it exists
to-day was unknown in Ireland till the year 1890, when
it was introduced into two British regiments. I notice
with amusement Stanihurst’s eulogy, which runs:—
“The chanter perforated in different places having 5 or
| 6 holes which the player so regulates by the dexterity
of the fingers that he can cause a loud or a low sound
at pleasure.” It’s mighty little music such a player
could evolve with his ‘ five or six holes,” seeing that the
great Highland chanter (as well as the Irish war pipe
chanter) with eight holes produces only nine notes. Not
one of the famous players from Courtenay to Talbot
ever handled an Irish war pipe, because the instrument

Catholics in South County Dublin have in the South
Dublin Catholic Insurance Society an axcellent means
of effecting their State Insurance. The large number
of proposal forms already submitted and accepted in-
dicates that the society will begin business in a strong
position, and, taken in conjunction with the healthy
nature of the district covered, ensures substantial
‘“ additional benefits ’’. after the Governmental valua-
tion, The society is strongly supported by the Catholic
clergy. Members are not submitted to any entrance
medical examination, and no subscription or entrance
fee whatever are charged. TFull particulars and names
of local agents may be had on application to the head
offices, No. 91 Lr. George’s Street, Kingstown, which
are open daily from 10 a.m. to 10 p.m.

AN IRISHWOMAN’S IMPRESSIONS OF PARIS.

T last T have reached the Mecca of my dreams—
Paris in June. Surely the words stand for all

that is best in art and beauty the world over. A blaz-
ing sun beats down on the Place de la Concorde, light-
ing in the distance the gilded dome of the Church of
the Invalides, shining on the brilliant green of the trees
of the Tuileries and the Champs Elysées. The gay
Parisienne, well decked in truth with all the most
modern artifice, drives by in every imaginable kind of
vehicle, all the world seems out of doors, while I linger
‘and muse and see ghosts between whiles. The appall-
ing days of the Reign of Terror come before me, the
Obelisk fades away, and I behold instead the scaffold

‘with its ugly guillotine on which Marie Antoinette was
_done to death, For me always one of the great attrac-

“ Juwy 6, 1912,

tions of the country with a history is the unseen pre-
sence of its ghosts. . . . . Meanwhile the crowd passes,
laughing, talking, flirting, and the whole avenue, from
the Champs Elysées to the Arc de Triomphe, anfi on
to the Bois, is alive with the joic de vwre. (/ufgzs
abound where men and women eat and drink together in
enjoyment and moderation, and from whose exumplc in
this respect I think we in Ireland might take a hint to
go and do likewise. v

1t is a conceit of mine always to picture Paris as a
lovely woman—of many moods biew entendu (did not

Alfred de Musset, who knew what he wrote about, say, °

‘ souvent femme varie ’), but a woman well beloved
through all her vagaries.

Thus musing I stroll on to the Church of the Inva-
lides, where all that is left of the *‘ Petit Caporal’”
reposes until the crack of doom. Even in death the
French have given him,of theiv best. He lies under
an enormous urn, in a erypt of polished granite, while
the whole chapel is flooded with a strange blue light
that comes from the stained glass windows above. At
the back are carved his own words, ‘‘Je désire que
mes cendres reposent sur les bords de la Seine au
milieu de ce peuple frangaise que j’ai tant aimé.”’

Hither wends the tourist from every country. His
presence (and hers) adds to that curious en féte appear-
ance which this portion of the gay city always presents.
In fact when in this quarter it is difficult to imagine
any hard work being done in Paris, except, perhaps,
watering the streets! That labour is incessant, and
the pedestrian has occasionally some trouble to avoid
a shower bath.

Some weeks ago when I arrived in the city of light T
looked to find a heathen land, with closed churches and
banished monks. Behold my preconceived ideas com-
pletely overthrown. It seems to me now that there are
more churches and more worshippers here than in Ire-
land, while T never walk abroad without encounteri
pious Abbé going on his round of duties or a ** Sister
going on hers, all wearing the insignia of their office in
the form of the gowns, crucifixes, beads, and veils with
which we are so familiar.

That France is very Catholic still, no one could doubs
who stood on the steps of the Madeline on the Sunday
following Corpus Christi and witnessed how the ** Fete
Dieu " was solemnized. To realize the perfect manner
in which the services were carried out ‘one should have
been present among the immense multitude gathered
together there. The band, the singing, the two Suisses
in gorgeous red and white livery and gold braid, the
four beadles in black and silver, the perfectly trained
boys, the High Mass—all produced an effect which no
words could convey. Afterwards came the Procession
of the Blessed Sacrament, consisting of priests, men,
and girls, while little boys, who came in twos alter-
nately, scattered rose leaves high in the air and swung
incense. The Benediction was given in the open from
the steps of the Madeline, while even those in the street
outside knelt down. All this shows that the Faith is
still strong in France.

That the Orders have been banished is a melancholy
fact, that the State no longer pays £37 a vear for the

HE work of Mr. Carl Hardebeck, of Belfast, for
the uplifting of Irish music is too well known
to readers of the LEADER to require any praise or com-
ment from me. Those who were fortunate enough to
obtain a copy of the ‘* Gems of Melody,”” Part 1., will
be glad to hear that a second part, containing nine
beautiful and characteristically Irish airs, arranged by
the same skilful hand, has just been issued from the
press.  Nobody who has not had to face audiences
whose acquaintance with Irish music was slight, some-
times with an accompanist at the piano, who regarded
Irish music as a thing utterly barbarous, knows what
a service Mr. Hardebeck has rendered to Irish singers.
The experiment was frequently tried of singing tra-
ditional airs without accompaniment, but though it
was successful with small audiences of exceptional
musical culture, or with audiences of Irish speakers;
before the ordinary mixed town or city audience it
““ did not take.”” The town or city audience demanded
a song with an accompaniment, but, curious fact, they
were not as a rule particular as to the kind of accom-
paniment, they got. Not so the singer, who often had
to struggle valiantly to give his own rendering of the

NATIONAL TEACHERS AND MATHEMATICS.

ET me, please, through the medium of your

popular paper, direct the attention of my fellow-

teachers and of all others who care, to a matter of
some importance.

We are told that the Commissioners are anxious for
higher-grade schools, for the advancement of educa-
tion, ete., but that the Treasury blocks the way. Good
old, handy, useful, blood-thirsty, man-eating Treasury !
The grown-ups’ Bogic Man! Like Jack-n-the-Chim-
ney for the youngsters, that blood-curdling ogre of a
Treasury hushes us adults up whenever we show signs
of being naughty, or symptoms of being noisy.  And
it does the frick every time. Men are but babies out
of swaddling-clothes.

Now, everyone will agree that for senior classes in
our boys’ schools in town or country, a good ground-
work in Mathematics is of the utmost importance. No
boy is, indeed, properly educated who has not gob a
good grip of at least the elementary principles of Al-
gebra, Geometry, and Mensuration. Whether you

look on the primary school as a feeder for the secon-
dary, or as the only scholastic training-ground for the
future farmer, artisan, soldier, or marine, in every case
you will admit that it does not properly fulfil its func-
tions if it does not give a training in Mathematics.

This admitted, the natural corollary is that those re-
sponsible for the ¢urriculwm in our primary schools,
and those anxious to foster and improve the education
imparted therein should do all that in them lies to
encourage the teaching of this subject.

How is the Irish teacher encouraged to take up the
teaching of Mathematics? Some years ago fees were
paid for extra subjects for every pupil (who passed the
test) who was present at fifty per cent. of the minimum
number of lessons required during a school year. The
pupils enrolled in Mathematics, being the chosen of the
Senior Division, and also those most anxious for learn-
ing (an anxiety which the study of Mathematics intensi-
fie§), would practically all qualify for fees under this
system. Hence, in an average country-school class of,
say, twelve pupils, the teacher could depend upon
getting fees for at least ten (10) of these, provided, of
course, he had taught ‘them properly. That was too
good a thing.  That method of payment was too fair.
Lt should not be. Down comes a rule that no longer
were fees to be paid for pupils individually, but only
for the class as a whole. The average attendance of
the class for the year was to be calculated, and then
the fees were to be paid at so much per caput of that
average. Seventy-five (75) per cent. is a pretty high
percentage of attendance for a class of grown-up country
boys in the Ireland of to-day The hours for extra
subjects (outside of school hours) are a little early on
short mornings, some will be late at the commence-
ment and consequently marked absent, inclement
weather will spoil the class for days at a time, and hur-
ried farm-work will play havoe with the attendance
of such pupils in the busy seasons. Consequently, an
average attendance of eight (8) out of our class of
twelve (12) will be rather above than below the mark.
The innocent-looking new rule encouraged the teacher
to take up the toilsome, laborious work of Mathematics
teaching by reducing his fees from the old style pay-
ment for ten to pagment for eight, or even less, for the
same work with the same class!

But this is not all. In our class of 12 (twelve), in
the old style, we allowed for two pupils who had not
made the attendances qualifying for fees. At the
examination by the inspector these two would not be
presented.  Only the ten who had qualified and for
whom the teacher might be held responsible (seeing
that they had been present for at least half of the
lessons during the year), only those ten stood the test

Now, for the unkindest cut of all. A few weeks
ago teachers were favoured with a circular to the effect
that even on a day when no pupil is present the rolls
must be marked and that day counted in computing the
average attendance for the year! Before this, bad as I
have shown the system was, a certain fixed minimum
number of lessons was required in the year. The teacher
might, in calculating the average, omit those days,
over and above the fixed minimum, on which the at-
tendance for any cause was unusually low. But we
bave changed all that. Now, all days must be entered
and included.

To-day the teacher may have ten present of his
twelve.  On to-morrow the flood-gates of heaven may
loosed. No pupil is present at the extra-early Mathe-
matics’ class.  The teacher must mark all absent,

° and count the day as one of the lesson-days of the
year. But he has the sublime satisfaction of feeling
that while on yesterday his class had the potential fee-
earning capacity of 10, to-day (averaging the attend-

“ance for both days) that very Irish god, Jupiter Pluvius,
has reduced the prospect of fees by half!

From experience I'know that in my district, and T
am sure my district is quite up to the average of other
country districts in Ireland (what with inclement
weather, with huried farm-work, with occasional reli-
gious exercises in the early morning in the neighbour-
ing church—and God forbid that we teachers in holy
Ireland will ever put sordid fees before the proper deve-
lopment of the Irish boys’ boasted spirituality)—I know
that there will be at least ten days in the year, there
may be up to twenty, when the attendance at the
Mathematics’ class of twelve (12) must be marked
from 0 to 2. Include these days with their sparse
attendances in the computation of the yearly average,
and you will find that the old-style payment for ten,

first cut down to a payment for eight or less, will have
dwindled to an almost negative quantity. That is

how the teaching of Mathematics in primary schools

The fees, even ab their best, were never sufficient
for the labour and scholarship required in the teaching
of these extensive courses. But they were somewhat
of an encouragement. As they stand at present, they
are an insult. The same work as of old must be done,
but the payment is not half of what it was. Merely
for the fees, even in their palmiest days, no teacher
would take up the subject. ~ He taught it because he
had care for his pupils, he had their interests at heart.
If fees he wanted, better for him to get a bee-hive and
let the bees change the nectar from his neighbours’
meadow-flowers into golden sovereigns at his feet. Bub

Can this sharp-practice, this mean cheese
laid at the doors of the Treasury too? I
doubt it. Tven if so, why did the Commissi
mit? Did they ever direct the attention of the public
to the matter? If not, why not? The Treasury—the
Bogie Man! Fiddlesticks!

Cannot the Central Executive Committee do any-
thing in the matter? What of the Managers' Associa-
tions ?

Fellow-teachers, for the benefit of your pupils, teach
Mathematics.  But insist upon being fairly treated.
End once for all this wretched cheese-paring, this con-
tinual application of Treasury soft-soap and sawdering-
iron.  Don’t be hushed up by stories about the Bogie
Man. Ask them to kindly pass the salt.

lings for the Working Classes, on site known

as to Buildings, and Sydney L. R. Price, Esq.,
Tlectrical Enginecer, as to IElectric Lighting. Plans,

Hall, Ballsbridge, and Bills of Quantities and Form

hours. The Urban Council will not be bound to
accept the lowest or any Tender, and it must he
distinetly understood that the sanction of the Local
Government Board and the approval of the Council

ceeded with. Amongst other matters, the Con-
1 , will provide that the Contractor shall be bound
as far as possible to supp and use articles of
Irish manufacture in the carrying out of the Con-
tract, and shall pay to those employed on the work
under his Contract not less than the minimum
standard rate of paid in Dublin and Dis-
trict, and to employ regular tradesmen to perform
tradesmen’s work and shall observe the hours and
conditions of employment now recognised as proper
in connection with each trade. That the work
executed under the Contract shall be done entirely
by local labour, and, where this is considered im- .
practicable, the Contractor shall apply to the Council
for permission to have the work done by other than
local labour, and the Council, having considered the
statements submitted by the Contractor, shall, by
resolution, determine whether the work is to be
done by local labour or otherwise. The Contractor
shall be bound by such resolution. In order to
ensure the better observance of the conditions of the

and fair wages, and as a further safeguard against

inspection at any time during working hours by a
duly authorised representative of “the trade affected .

officer of the Council. Tenders to be sent in so

ance .of the Contract, which Contract will be pre-
pared by the Council’s Law Agents at the expense
